topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot groaned open with a weary sigh, depositing a flimsy piece of cardstock into my waiting hand. It was a birthday card, utterly blank save for a single, smudged scribble in the corner. I stared at it, my jaw tightening. Another one. I pinched the bridge of my nose, the skin there protesting the pressure. It was Tuesday. Tuesday, and I was supposed to be focusing on quarterly reports, not deciphering the cryptic intentions of unknown card-writers. I crumpled the paper slightly, the sound sharp in the quiet apartment. Why did the universe insist on sending these unsolicited, pointless gestures? I tossed it onto the cluttered desk, a gesture of pure, unadulterated defeat.

[story 2]

I picked up the card with two fingers, turning it over and over, my brow furrowing into a deep V. The ink was faint, a hesitant attempt at personalization that amounted to nothing. "Seriously?" I muttered under my breath, the sound laced with a dry, brittle quality. I let the card drop onto the floor, where it lay amidst old receipts and dried-out pens. My shoulders slumped forward, a heavy weight settling across my back. This was the third such item this month. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the living room twice before stopping dead by the window, staring out at the indifferent street. I needed a vacation, or perhaps just a complete cessation of receiving physical mail.

[story 3]

The envelope was thin, the paper slightly warped from the humidity. Inside, the card offered nothing—no name, no message, just a faint impression of handwriting that looked like it was written in haste. I held it up to the light, scrutinizing the void. A low, frustrated hum vibrated in my chest. I let out a slow, controlled breath, the kind you take when trying desperately to maintain composure while the world seems determined to unravel. I shook my head once, sharply, dismissing the object entirely. With a decisive movement, I slid the card back into the box, sealing it with a firm, final snap. It was done. No more.
